answer:
## Tóm tắt nhanh

Tôi đã đọc kỹ corpus đã verify (có cả phán quyết refuted). Trả lời dưới đây trung thực theo đúng những gì source AGPL của Warp và docs Anthropic xác nhận, và flag rõ chỗ refuted/uncertain.

---

## 1. Warp input editor hoạt động chính xác thế nào + cơ chế chuyển raw-mode khi gặp TUI

**Kiến trúc input editor.** Warp's "modern command editor" là một GUI text editor đầy đủ (rope/SumTree-backed, GPU-rendered bằng WarpUI framework riêng) chạy **hoàn toàn trong process của Warp**, không đụng tới readline/zle của shell. Keystrokes bị Warp GUI bắt và **không gửi xuống PTY cho tới khi user nhấn Enter**; lúc đó cả dòng lệnh mới được ghi xuống PTY ([how-warp-works](https://www.warp.dev/blog/how-warp-works), [why-is-the-terminal-input-so-weird](https://www.warp.dev/blog/why-is-the-terminal-input-so-weird)). Đây là điểm cốt lõi giải thích tại sao input box "tách rời" được khỏi output: shell vẫn ở canonical mode, Warp chỉ buffer dòng lệnh ở app layer.

**State machine quyết định ẩn/hiện input box.** Source AGPL (`app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs:1008-1025`) có enum 4 trạng thái, và hàm `terminal_input_state()`:

```rust
pub enum TerminalInputState { AltScreen, InputEditor, LongRunningCommand, NotBootstrapped }
// AltScreen      → TUI (vim/htop) đang chiếm màn → input editor BỊ ẨN
// LongRunningCommand → lệnh primary-screen đang chạy lâu → input editor cũng ẩn, key forward thẳng
// InputEditor    → prompt shell bình thường → input box HIỆN
```

**Signal trigger chính xác là DECSET 1049/47 — KHÔNG phải PTY raw-mode (ICANON off).** Đây là điểm quan trọng và đã được verify **confirmed (high)**. Warp tự parse VT stream (có `impl ansi::Handler for TerminalModel`), khi gặp `ESC[?1049h`/`ESC[?1047h`/`ESC[?47h` → `Mode::SwapScreen` → `enter_alt_screen()` set `alt_screen_active = true` và bắn `TerminalModeSwapped(AltScreen)`; render dispatch chuyển từ `BlockListElement` sang `AltScreenElement` (full raw grid), `is_input_box_visible()` trả `false` ([control_sequence_parameters.rs:90-163](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/warpdotdev/Warp@a44b703/-/blob/crates/warp_terminal/src/model/ansi/control_sequence_parameters.rs), [terminal_model.rs:2033-2078](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/warpdotdev/Warp@a44b703/-/blob/app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs)).

Một chương trình đặt tty raw **mà không gửi 1049** sẽ KHÔNG vào `AltScreen` — nó rơi vào `LongRunningCommand` (cơ chế **time-based** `is_active_and_long_running()`, `block.rs:1717-1752`, không hề check termios/ICANON). Nghĩa là: Warp **không** có cơ chế detect raw-mode; nó thuần dựa vào ANSI byte sequence. Không có "raw passthrough mode" riêng — chỉ là một render path khác (alt-screen grid).

---

## 2. Warp + Claude Code: input box hay raw TUI?

**Chạy `claude` trong Warp → bạn vẫn thấy chính TUI của Claude Code; Warp KHÔNG thay thế nó.** Warp auto-detect CLI agent (qua command name + plugin `claude-code-warp`), rồi **bọc thêm** chrome xung quanh: agent footer/toolbelt + một rich-input overlay mở bằng **Ctrl-G**. TUI của Claude Code (input line, spinner, tool-use blocks, diff) vẫn tự render vào surface như ở bất kỳ terminal nào. Bằng chứng coexistence: issue [#9365](https://github.com/warpdotdev/warp/issues/9365) (input box render chen vào giữa output CC), [#9179](https://github.com/warpdotdev/warp/issues/9179) (Shift+Tab của CC bị Warp Rich Input nuốt), [#11644](https://github.com/warpdotdev/warp/issues/11644) (mất focus) — những bug này chỉ tồn tại nếu hai hệ thống cùng vẽ lên một surface.

**Ctrl-G rich input submit thế nào — verify confirmed (high), và đã sửa một claim cũ.** Có hai con đường khác nhau trong source, đừng nhầm:
- **Chạy `claude` tương tác trong shell:** Ctrl-G mở overlay Warp-native, compose prompt, rồi **ghi thẳng bytes xuống cùng OS PTY** mà Claude Code đọc (`submit_cli_agent_rich_input` → `write_cli_agent_text_then_submit` → `write_user_bytes_to_pty` → `PtyController::write_bytes`). Với Claude dùng chiến lược **DelayedEnter**: ghi text trước, sau ~50ms (`CLI_AGENT_PTY_WRITE_DELAY`) mới ghi `\r`. **KHÔNG có IPC/WebSocket/SDK channel riêng** — chỉ là PTY write giả lập gõ phím ([use_agent_footer/mod.rs:638-719,105-143](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/warpdotdev/Warp@a44b703/-/blob/app/src/terminal/view/use_agent_footer/mod.rs)).
- **Oz/Agent Mode launch Claude headless:** Warp ghi prompt ra temp file rồi `claude --session-id <uuid> --dangerously-skip-permissions < '<prompt_path>'` (stdin redirect, non-interactive) — đây là con đường riêng của Warp's own agent.

> **CẢI CHÍNH một claim trong corpus:** Bản tổng hợp AI cũ nói submit dùng "DelayedEnter" như một harness PTY-injection generic — **đúng tên DelayedEnter** (xác nhận trong `use_agent_footer`) nhưng **`harness_support.rs` KHÔNG phải file injection**; nó là CLI dispatch cho Oz. Hai verifier mâu thuẫn nhẹ ở chi tiết file nhưng **thống nhất kết luận**: submit = PTY write, không có channel riêng.

**`warpdotdev/claude-code-warp` chỉ là notification bridge, KHÔNG cho input box.** Plugin map 6 hooks (SessionStart, Stop, Notification/idle_prompt, PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse) → emit OSC 777 `\033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<JSON>\007`. Đây là kênh **một chiều, outbound-only** để Warp hiện notification + tab metadata. Không mang prompt text theo chiều nào ([claude-code-warp](https://github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp), [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp/3-how-it-works)). CC >= 2.1.141 có thể trả `terminalSequence` JSON thay vì ghi `/dev/tty` (chạy được headless/remote).

**Warp Agent Mode (Oz) vs chạy Claude Code CLI — hai sản phẩm tách biệt:**

| | Claude Code CLI trong Warp | Warp Agent Mode (Oz) |
|---|---|---|
| Ai execute | process `claude` trong PTY | agent built-in của Warp |
| Input UI | TUI riêng của CC + overlay Ctrl-G của Warp | input editor + tool-call cards **native** WarpUI |
| Tool cards | do CC tự vẽ vào PTY | native cards (diff viewer, model selector...) |
| Auth | Anthropic Max/API key (của bạn) | Warp AI credits / BYOK enterprise |

Native UI (input box + tool-call cards thật) **chỉ có cho Oz**. CLI agent ngoài (Claude Code/Codex/Gemini) chỉ được lớp harness nhẹ (footer + Ctrl-G), KHÔNG được native tool-call cards ([reimagining-ADE blog](https://www.warp.dev/blog/reimagining-coding-agentic-development-environment), [warp-vs-claude-code](https://docs.warp.dev/guides/agent-workflows/warp-vs-claude-code/)).

**Claude Code có dùng alt-screen không? — claim cũ "không bao giờ" bị REFUTED (high).** CC có **hai mode**: (1) **classic** (mặc định lịch sử): stream inline ra primary scrollback, **không** DECSET 1049 → trong Warp là `LongRunningCommand`; (2) **fullscreen** (opt-in từ v2.1.89 qua `/tui fullscreen` hoặc `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1`): **dùng alt-screen như vim/htop** → trong Warp là `AltScreen`, input editor block-mode bị ẩn ([fullscreen docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen), issue [#42670](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/42670)). Env opt-out: `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1` (thêm ~v2.1.132).

> **UNCERTAIN, load-bearing:** Liệu fullscreen đã thành **mặc định** ở các bản gần đây chưa? Việc thêm env opt-out ở v2.1.132 gợi ý đang rollout dần thành default, nhưng docs vẫn ghi "opt-in research preview". **Phải test thực nghiệm**, đừng giả định. Test: `script -q /dev/null claude 2>&1 | xxd | grep "1049"` — thấy `\x1b[?1049h` = đang fullscreen/alt-screen.

---

## 3. Trên stack libghostty của ta: làm được ô input ngoài kiểu Warp không?

Trước hết, sự thật phũ về **opacity của libghostty** (verify confirmed, high): surface API `ghostty.h` chỉ có 6 callback (`wakeup_cb`, `action_cb`, clipboard×3, `close_surface_cb`); `action_cb` bắn TITLE/PWD/COMMAND_FINISHED/PROGRESS/SCROLLBAR/desktop-notif — **KHÔNG có** `GHOSTTY_ACTION_ALT_SCREEN_ENTERED`, **KHÔNG có** hàm query screen mode. Hằng `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN` **chỉ tồn tại trong sub-library `libghostty-vt`** (`include/ghostty/vt/terminal.h`), **không reach được qua surface API**. Muốn dùng nó bạn phải **link riêng libghostty-vt, tự tạo `ghostty_terminal_t`, feed song song raw VT bytes** — tức chạy một state machine VT thứ hai bên cạnh renderer ([ghostty.h:884-1181](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty@0f7cd84b/-/blob/include/ghostty.h), [vt/terminal.h:660-666](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty@0f7cd84b/-/blob/include/ghostty/vt/terminal.h)).

**(a) Shell block mode — KHẢ THI.** Đây đúng là mô hình Warp. Bạn sở hữu PTY: render input box native (SwiftUI) bên ngoài surface, Enter → ghi cả dòng xuống PTY master, output stream vẫn vẽ trong ghostty surface phía trên. Effort ~**2-4 tuần**. Hai chỗ khó: (i) giấu echo của shell để không đè input box (dùng shell integration / quản echo); (ii) multi-line + history.

> **CẢI CHÍNH (REFUTED, high):** Đừng tin claim "Warp dùng OSC 133 để chia block". **Warp KHÔNG dùng OSC 133** — nó dùng protocol DCS/OSC riêng + JSON, driven bởi hooks precmd/preexec; thậm chí OSC 133 do user tự chèn **làm Warp vỡ** (issue [#6718](https://github.com/warpdotdev/warp/issues/6718)). Với stack của bạn: cách thực dụng nhất để bắt block boundary **không** phải bắt chước Warp mà là dùng chính **`GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED`** (có `exit_code` + `duration`) từ action_cb — đây là tín hiệu libghostty đã cho sẵn, không cần parse OSC 133. Input editor (ô input tách rời) là feature app-layer, **không phụ thuộc shell hook nào**.

**(b) TUI passthrough + detect alt-screen — KHẢ THI nhưng cần tự parse byte stream.** Vì surface API không báo alt-screen, bạn phải **sniff PTY byte stream TRƯỚC khi đưa vào `ghostty_surface_text()`**, quét đúng ~6 sequence cố định: `ESC[?1049h/l`, `ESC[?1047h/l`, `ESC[?47h/l`. Khi thấy `h` → set `altScreenActive=true`, ẩn input box, forward phím raw; thấy `l` → đảo lại. Đây **chính xác** là cơ chế Warp (Warp tự là parser; bạn cũng phải tự là parser ở tầng byte). Đây là parser nhỏ, fixed-length, không cần parse full VT. Effort ~**1-2 tuần** sau khi (a) xong. (Phương án thay thế dùng libghostty-vt song song nặng hơn nhiều — không khuyến nghị chỉ để detect alt-screen.)

**(c) Claude Code qua SDK pane — ĐÚNG hướng cho UX native, EFFORT CAO.** Vì CC interactive là TUI (classic = redraw primary screen char-by-char raw; fullscreen = alt-screen), một "ô input thật sự tách rời + cards" **không thể** coexist sạch — overlay sẽ vỡ key handling hoặc chỉ là lớp pre-compose rồi đổ text vào TUI (đúng kiểu Ctrl-G của Warp, fragile, phải đúng lúc CC ở prompt). Con đường sạch: chạy `claude -p --output-format stream-json --verbose --include-partial-messages`, parse NDJSON events (`system/init`, `assistant`+`text_delta`, `tool_use`, `tool_result`, `result`), render từng loại thành card native, input box native feed stdin. CC **không** hiện TUI. Effort ~**4-8 tuần** (đây là viết một frontend Claude Code, không phải wrap CLI). Lưu ý billing: Agent SDK metered riêng so với interactive trên subscription plans ([headless docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless), [SDK docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/sdk)).

---

## 4. Khuyến nghị thực tế (kiến trúc hybrid)

Cho user cái ô-input-ngoài họ thích, theo đúng cái Warp thực sự làm năm 2026:

**A. Shell commands → ô input native + block mode.**
- Render SwiftUI input box pinned bottom; Enter → ghi dòng xuống PTY; dùng `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED` (action_cb có sẵn) để vẽ ranh giới block. Không cần OSC 133.
- **Bắt buộc** thêm parser byte-stream sniff `ESC[?1049h/l` (+1047/47) ở read loop NetBird→trước ghostty, để ẩn/hiện input box và đổi key-forwarding. Đây là phần (b) ở trên — nó là điều kiện cần để input box không đè vim/htop/btop.

**B. Claude Code → tách thành HAI lựa chọn rõ ràng cho user:**
- **B1 (coexistence, kiểu Warp Ctrl-G):** giữ TUI của CC trong ghostty surface, thêm overlay native compose-box, submit = PTY write (giả lập gõ) + DelayedEnter. Rẻ nhưng **fragile** (đúng lúc CC ở prompt; xung đột Shift+Tab/focus như issue Warp). Không có tool-call cards native.
- **B2 (SDK pane, kiểu Oz):** không chạy TUI, drive CC qua stream-json, render cards native, ô input là input thật. Đắt nhưng đây mới là "Warp-style thật sự" cho agent. Đây là con đường duy nhất cho native input box + tool cards.

**Khuyến nghị của tôi:** làm **A + B2**. A cho trải nghiệm shell Warp-style ngay (ROI cao, rủi ro thấp). B2 cho trải nghiệm Claude Code native đúng cái user mê ở Warp — vì ngay cả Warp cũng phải bypass TUI (dùng Oz/SDK) để có native cards; overlay-trên-TUI (B1) ở Warp vẫn đang sinh bug. B1 chỉ nên làm nếu cần "có ngay" trong lúc B2 chưa xong.

**Các điểm CẦN USER QUYẾT:**
1. **CC fullscreen có là default trên target version không?** (uncertain — phải test bằng `script ... | grep 1049`). Quyết định này đổi việc parser alt-screen có bắt được CC interactive hay không.
2. **Có cần giữ CC interactive TUI cho user "muốn full TUI" không?** Nếu có cả TUI passthrough lẫn SDK pane → effort gần gấp đôi.
3. **Auth cho B2:** SDK chạy bằng subscription OAuth của user (giống bạn đang dùng) — xác nhận flow này hoạt động headless trên remote host (NetBird) trước khi cam kết B2.
4. **Shell integration trên remote host:** nếu không cài được hook, vẫn ổn — fallback `COMMAND_FINISHED` của libghostty đủ cho block boundary; chỉ mất metadata giàu (git/pwd/exit annotations).

**Lưu ý nhỏ:** `TERM=xterm-ghostty` bạn set **không liên quan** tới toàn bộ logic mode-switch này (mode switch thuần là ANSI bytes), và `xterm-ghostty` cũng nằm trong danh sách terminal CC nhận diện cho Shift+Enter — một điểm cộng nhỏ.

corpus.summary: Warp's "modern command editor" is a full Rust GUI text editor (rope/SumTree-backed, GPU-rendered via their custom warpui framework) that lives in the client process and never touches the PTY for keystroke-by-keystroke editing. The terminal model holds a hard-coded state machine — TerminalInputState — with four values: InputEditor, AltScreen, LongRunningCommand, NotBootstrapped. The trigger for hiding the input box is ANSI DECSET 1049 (or 47), which Warp parses as Mode::SwapScreen and routes to enter_alt_screen(), which sets alt_screen_active = true and fires TerminalModeSwapped(AltScreen) on the event bus. Every UI subscriber (input view, use_agent_footer, etc.) calls notify_and_notify_children and re-renders: the input editor disappears and the alt-screen grid fills the terminal. On DECRST 1049 the reverse happens. For Claude Code specifically — which runs on the primary screen, not alt-screen — Warp takes a different path: it detects Claude via the claude-code-warp plugin (OSC 777 / warp:// events) plus command-pattern detection, opens a CLIAgentInputState::Open session, and renders render_cli_agent_input (an Ctrl-G-triggered overlay editor pinned at the bottom) *on top of* the running Claude Code TUI. The key asymmetry is that Claude Code does NOT use alt-screen; it redraws on the primary screen. Warp therefore keeps the overlay editor visible alongside the Claude Code output, color-matching the overlay background to Claude's inferred background color. Replicating this on the libghostty stack for shell commands is feasible (the DECSET 1049 trigger is available via the action callback stream); for Claude Code specifically, a Warp-style separate input box almost certainly means bypassing the Claude Code TUI and driving it via the Agent SDK (stream-JSON), not patching over its primary-screen TUI.

points.claim: The input editor is a full GUI text editor running entirely in the Warp client process, never using shell readline
points.detail: Warp built a 'full-fledged text editor as our input editor as an editor view in our framework' (Nathan Sobo, how-warp-works blog). It uses a SumTree (Rope variant) to hold the command buffer, supports multiple cursors, soft-wrap, vim keybindings, syntax highlighting, and IDE-style cursor movement. Keystrokes are intercepted by the Warp GUI and never go to the PTY until the user presses Enter. Shell readline/zle is bypassed entirely — the shell is launched with shell integration hooks (precmd/preexec for zsh/fish; bash-preexec for bash) that disable the shell's own line editing and instruct it to just exec whatever it receives via the PTY write after Enter. The shell integration sends a DCS (Device Control String) JSON payload to Warp with session metadata on each precmd event, which Warp uses to create a new block boundary.

points.claim: The mode-switching trigger is ANSI DECSET 1049 (or 47), decoded as Mode::SwapScreen, which calls enter_alt_screen() and sets alt_screen_active = true
points.detail: In crates/warp_terminal/src/model/ansi/control_sequence_parameters.rs: '1049 => Mode::SwapScreen { save_cursor_and_clear_screen: true }' and '47 => Mode::SwapScreen { save_cursor_and_clear_screen: false }'. In terminal_model.rs set_mode(), Mode::SwapScreen calls self.enter_alt_screen(). enter_alt_screen() sets self.alt_screen_active = true, resets the alt-screen grid, copies the primary-screen cursor, and fires Event::TerminalModeSwapped(TerminalMode::AltScreen) on the event proxy. The inverse — DECRST 1049 — calls exit_alt_screen(), sets alt_screen_active = false, restores the cursor, and fires TerminalModeSwapped(TerminalMode::BlockList). This is the exclusive trigger; Warp does NOT watch for the PTY going into raw/ICANON-off mode as a separate signal.

points.claim: The TerminalInputState enum is the master switch that drives input-box visibility: four values, AltScreen hides the editor
points.detail: pub enum TerminalInputState { AltScreen ('Alt-screen on which programs like vim run is visible'), InputEditor ('Warp Input View is visible'), LongRunningCommand ('Block-list is visible but input will go to the running command'), NotBootstrapped }. terminal_input_state() checks: not bootstrapped → NotBootstrapped; is_alt_screen_active() == true → AltScreen; active_block is_active_and_long_running() → LongRunningCommand; else → InputEditor. The Input::render() method at input.rs:15348 is what actually dispatches to the right render path. When TerminalModeSwapped is fired, every subscribed UI component calls notify_and_notify_children(ctx) causing a full re-render — the input box ceases to be rendered when state == AltScreen.

points.claim: Full-screen TUIs using alt-screen (vim, top, less, htop, etc.) cause the input box to vanish; LongRunningCommand (e.g. a blocking primary-screen process) does not hide it but disables completions
points.detail: Programs that use DECSET 1049 (vim, top, less, btop, etc.) trigger TerminalInputState::AltScreen, which fully hides the input editor — the alt-screen grid fills the entire pane. Programs that run on the primary screen but block input (e.g. a long curl, a build process, ssh without alt-screen) trigger TerminalInputState::LongRunningCommand. In this state the input editor is still rendered but completions are disabled (input.rs:11401-11404 checks !model.is_alt_screen_active() && active_block.is_command_grid_active() before enabling completions). Raw mode alone (ICANON off, no alt-screen) does NOT trigger the mode switch in Warp's model — it is purely DECSET 1049/47 driven.

points.claim: Claude Code runs on the primary screen (NOT alt-screen), so Warp does NOT hide the input box when Claude Code is running — instead it renders an overlay CLI-agent rich input on top
points.detail: Claude Code's TUI renders frames on the primary screen via clear-and-redraw sequences (ClearMode::All + cursor moves). Warp's tui-output-redraw spec document (specs/tui-output-redraw/TECH.md) explicitly states: 'Claude Code and similar CLI agents run pseudo-TUI interfaces in the primary screen.' Because alt_screen_active stays false during Claude Code sessions, TerminalInputState stays LongRunningCommand (the active block is a long-running command). The separate Ctrl-G editor shown in the Warp UI for Claude Code is the CLIAgentInputState::Open path — input.rs:15349 checks CLIAgentSessionsModel::is_input_open() first, before checking TerminalInputState, and if open renders render_cli_agent_input (a distinct editor pinned at the bottom, with max height CLI_AGENT_RICH_INPUT_EDITOR_MAX_HEIGHT, top/bottom padding constants). This editor color-matches its background to the inferred background of the Claude Code terminal output (terminal_model.alt_screen().inferred_bg_color() is checked — but since Claude is on primary screen, the code actually falls through to using the default Warp theme when no alt-screen bg is found). The render_cli_agent_input path includes attachment chips, agent footer, and slash-command menus.

points.claim: Warp detects Claude Code via two mechanisms: the claude-code-warp plugin sending OSC 777 / warp:// events, and command-pattern detection on the executed command string
points.detail: The plugin_manager/claude.rs sets PLUGIN_KEY = 'warp@claude-code-warp', MARKETPLACE_REPO = 'warpdotdev/claude-code-warp', MINIMUM_PLUGIN_VERSION = '2.1.0'. Warp auto-installs the claude-code-warp plugin by checking the Claude home dir (claude_home_dir()) for the plugin files. When Claude Code starts, the plugin sends hook events (SessionStart, Stop, Notification, PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse) as OSC 777 escape sequences with JSON payloads to warp://cli-agent. Warp's CLIAgentSessionListener processes these and updates the CLIAgentSession state. Sessions can also be created by command detection alone (the custom_command_prefix field on CLIAgentSession), without the plugin. The listener's is_agent_supported() / create_handler() factory dispatches specifically on CLIAgent::Claude.

points.claim: Warp 2.0 (April 2026): repo went AGPL, Claude Code path is now called 'cli-agents' with a dedicated render path and Ctrl-G UX; Warp's own Agent Mode is entirely separate from running Claude Code CLI
points.detail: Warp's own Agent Mode uses render_agent_input with a full conversation view (model selector, voice input, image attachment). The CLI-agent path (render_cli_agent_input via Ctrl-G) is a lighter editor pinned at the bottom for typing prompts that get forwarded to the running Claude Code process via PTY write. These are two entirely separate code paths. The Warp 2.0 announcement added 'Oz', a cloud orchestrator for background agents, which is another separate product. When you run Claude Code in Warp you are using the cli-agents path, NOT Warp's native Agent Mode. Claude Code's own TUI occupies the block output area; the Ctrl-G box is an optional overlay for composing the next prompt without having to click into the terminal.

points.claim: Feasibility for your libghostty stack: Warp-style separate input box for shell commands is straightforward; for Claude Code it requires bypassing the TUI
points.detail: For shell commands (block mode): the DECSET 1049 entry/exit events are well-defined VT sequences. Your libghostty surface fires action callbacks including COMMAND_FINISHED and PWD/TITLE/PROGRESS. You can detect alt-screen entry by watching for the ESC[?1049h byte sequence in the PTY output stream (which libghostty passes through as-is). On that trigger hide the native input box and route keystrokes raw to the PTY. On ESC[?1049l restore it. This is the exact same logic Warp uses. For shell command mode (normal non-TUI), building a native input box that writes the final command to the PTY on Enter is straightforward — the shell is already in canonical mode. For Claude Code specifically: Claude Code's TUI renders on the primary screen and constantly redraws frames — a Warp-style 'box alongside it' means either (a) the Ctrl-G trick where you overlay a native box and PTY-write to Claude's stdin (what Warp does — you type a prompt, hit Enter, the text goes to the PTY as if you typed it in the terminal), or (b) fully replace the TUI by driving Claude Code via the Agent SDK (stream-JSON mode, sdk.run()) and building a native input+cards UI. Option (a) is low-complexity but fragile (Claude Code must be at its prompt); option (b) is the correct long-term path for a truly native experience.

corpus.project_relevance: For the native-Swift remote-coding tool: (1) Input box for shell commands — detect ESC[?1049h/l in the byte stream (libghostty passes it through); hide/show the native input box accordingly. (2) LongRunningCommand analog — you already have COMMAND_FINISHED callbacks; the inverse (command started, not finished) is your signal to disable the native input box completions but keep it visible. verdict.claim: Warp's mode switch (input-editor → full-screen passthrough) is triggered exclusively by DECSET 1049/47 (the VT SwapScreen sequence) and NOT by the PTY going raw (ICANON off). A custom ncurses app that puts the tty raw without using alt-screen would NOT hide the Warp input box.
verdict.evidence:
The Warp source (open-sourced AGPL April 2026, ~56k stars) provides direct confirmation across several files. The full mechanism is:

**1. The trigger is exclusively DECSET 1049/47 — source-confirmed.**

`crates/warp_terminal/src/model/ansi/control_sequence_parameters.rs:101-146` defines the only two modes that activate the alternate screen:
- `?47` → `SwapScreen { save_cursor_and_clear_screen: false }`
- `?1049` → `SwapScreen { save_cursor_and_clear_screen: true }`

`app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs:2660-2666` — `set_mode()` calls `enter_alt_screen()` exclusively on receipt of `SwapScreen`. There is no PTY raw-mode detection path.

`enter_alt_screen()` (lines 2033-2078) sets `self.alt_screen_active = true` and fires `Event::TerminalModeSwapped(TerminalMode::AltScreen)`.

**2. The `TerminalInputState` state machine drives what is shown/hidden.**

`terminal_model.rs:1611-1624` (function `terminal_input_state`):
```rust
pub fn terminal_input_state(&self) -> TerminalInputState {
    if !self.block_list().is_bootstrapped() {
        TerminalInputState::NotBootstrapped
    } else if self.is_alt_screen_active() {
        TerminalInputState::AltScreen          // <- DECSET 1049 only
    } else if self.block_list().active_block().is_active_and_long_running() {
        TerminalInputState::LongRunningCommand // <- time-based, not raw-mode
    } else {
        TerminalInputState::InputEditor        // <- Warp input box visible
    }
}
```

States:
- `AltScreen` — full-screen TUI content visible, input editor hidden. Triggered by DECSET 1049/47 only.
- `LongRunningCommand` — block-list visible, input editor hidden, keystrokes forwarded to the running process. Triggered when `is_active_and_long_running()` is true.
- `InputEditor` — Warp input box is visible and editable.

**3. `LongRunningCommand` is time-based, not PTY-raw-mode-based.**

`block.rs:1717-1752` — `is_active_and_long_running()` returns true when the block is in `BlockState::Executing` and `start_time.elapsed().as_millis() >= LONG_RUNNING_COMMAND_DURATION_MS`. It caches the result once true. There is NO check of PTY ICANON flags, termios, or raw mode anywhere in this path. The comment on line 1718 explicitly states: "We use this to determine whether to hide the input box."

**4. Adversarial refutation: raw-mode-without-alt-screen does NOT hide the input box.**

A program that calls `cfmakeraw()` / sets `~ICANON` on the PTY slave without sending `\e[?1049h` will stay in `LongRunningCommand` state (if the time threshold passes) — meaning Warp still shows the block-list and routes all keystrokes through to the running process, but the Warp input box stays hidden only because the command is still running, not because of any raw-mode detection. If the command finishes quickly (before the LRC threshold) Warp would show the input box again. The alt-screen branch requires the actual VT sequence.

**5. What Claude Code runs as inside Warp.**

Claude Code's default renderer ("classic") writes inline to the primary screen, so Warp treats it as `LongRunningCommand`. As of v2.1.89+, the opt-in fullscreen mode (`CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1` / `/tui fullscreen`) sends `\e[?1049h`, switching Warp to `AltScreen` state — at which point Warp hides the input editor and renders the `AltScreenElement` instead.

The GitHub issue #9365 ("Input prompt box renders mid-output during long-running TUI sessions") confirms: when Claude Code uses alt-screen + heavy streaming, Warp's input-box positioning logic can race with the TUI redraws — a bug specific to Claude Code's high-throughput streaming pattern, not reproducible in vim/htop/lazygit.

**6. The claude-code-warp integration (OSC 777 / warp://cli-agent).**

`warpdotdev/claude-code-warp` is a Claude Code plugin that fires hooks (PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse) and emits OSC 777 structured JSON payloads addressed to `warp://cli-agent`. This drives Warp's notification UI and tab-bar indicators. It does NOT change the rendering mode — Claude Code's TUI still owns the screen in its current mode.

**7. Feasibility of Warp-style input box on a libghostty stack.**

The Warp model is: parse the VT stream → detect `SwapScreen` → switch state machine → render either `AltScreenElement` or `InputEditor+BlockList`. On a libghostty surface (opaque VT renderer), you cannot intercept the VT parse. You would need to either:
(a) Parse the PTY byte stream yourself before passing to ghostty's surface, watching for `\e[?1049h` / `\e[?1049l`, or
(b) Use ghostty's `COMMAND_FINISHED` / `PWD` callbacks as a proxy signal for when to show the input box (block-mode only).
For the Claude Code case specifically: the TUI mode owns the screen. A native Warp-style input box for Claude Code interaction would require bypassing the TUI entirely — driving Claude Code via the `--output-format stream-json` / `--print` Agent SDK flags into a native UI pane, not running its interactive TUI at all.
verdict.correction: N/A — claim confirmed. The one nuance: a raw-mode-without-1049 program will still cause the input editor to hide via the `LongRunningCommand` time-based path (if it runs longer than the threshold), but this is a different mechanism and the switch back to `InputEditor` happens when the command exits — not driven by PTY raw-mode detection at all.

The submit path is: `submit_cli_agent_rich_input` (use_agent_footer/mod.rs:638) → `write_cli_agent_text_then_submit` (mod.rs:943) → `write_user_bytes_to_pty` → `view::Event::WriteBytesToPty` / `WriteAgentInputToPty` → `PtyController::write_bytes` → the OS PTY fd. verdict.evidence:
The claim is refuted by strong, authoritative evidence. Claude Code v2.1.89 (released ~April 2026) introduced a fullscreen rendering mode that explicitly uses DECSET 1049 (the alternate screen buffer). The official Claude Code docs state: "Fullscreen rendering…draws the interface on the terminal's alternate screen buffer, like vim or htop." The env var CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1 reverts to the classic renderer. Issue #42670 filed against v2.1.90 confirms "Claude Code switched to an alternate screen buffer (fullscreen TUI) starting in v2.1.89." As of current versions, fullscreen mode is opt-in (run /tui fullscreen or set CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1) but persists across sessions once set. The classic renderer (no alt-screen, output goes to scrollback) remains available.

---

## (1) How Warp's input editor works and how it coexists with full-screen TUI programs

### Architecture

Warp is written in Rust with its own GPU-accelerated UI framework (WarpUI, now MIT-licensed). The terminal pane renders as a Flex::column with two stacked regions:
- `Shrinkable::new(1., output_area)` — the block list, flex-factor 1, fills all remaining vertical space
- `render_input()` — the input editor, intrinsic height, pinned to the bottom

This is confirmed by the internal tech spec at [specs/cloud-mode-input-v2/TECH.md:139-144](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/warpdotdev/Warp@a44b703060673b85d2641c051c53e4b6b1b00cc4/-/blob/specs/cloud-mode-input-v2/TECH.md) in the open-source repo. The input editor is a full IDE-style editor backed by a SumTree (rope) data structure, designed as an operation-based CRDT for future collaboration. Blocks (groupings of command + output) are created via precmd/preexec DCS hooks.

### Behavior when a full-screen TUI (vim, htop, etc.) runs

When a process sends DECSET 1049 (enter alternate screen), Warp:
1. Switches the terminal model from `block_list` mode to `alt_screen_active = true` (confirmed by `TerminalModel::enter_alt_screen()` and `alt_screen_active: bool` field in [terminal_model.rs:452-465](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/warpdotdev/Warp@a44b703060673b85d2641c051c53e4b6b1b00cc4/-/blob/app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs))
2. The `output_area` renders `AltScreenElement` (confirmed by `alt_screen_element.rs` in `app/src/terminal/alt_screen/`) instead of the block list
3. The standard shell input editor is hidden — `is_input_box_visible()` controls this and checks `is_alt_screen_active()` (confirmed by the TerminalView render logic in `specs/cloud-mode-input-v2/TECH.md:166-173` which reads: `if model.is_alt_screen_active() && self.should_render_use_agent_footer(&model, app)`)
4. Mouse events are passed through to the running app via SGR mouse sequences when mouse tracking is enabled (confirmed by `alt_screen/mod.rs:should_intercept_mouse`)
5. The Kitty keyboard protocol is supported for extended key encoding

The TerminalView render pseudocode from the spec is definitive:
```rust
column.add_child(Shrinkable::new(1., output_area));   // block list OR alt_screen_element
if model.is_alt_screen_active() && self.should_render_use_agent_footer(&model, app) {
    column.add_child(ChildView::new(&self.use_agent_footer).finish());
}
if self.is_input_box_visible(&model, app) {
    column.add_child(self.render_input());
}
```

So the Warp input editor DOES disappear during alt-screen. The one exception: when a CLI agent (e.g. Claude Code with Warp integration) has its rich input open on top of an alt-screen process, the rich input editor stays visible. This is deliberately handled by `cli_agent.rs:update_cli_agent_editor_text_colors` which adjusts text contrast against the alt-screen app's inferred background color.

There is no simple "raw passthrough" — Warp always parses the VT stream; it just renders the alt-screen grid directly instead of via blocks.

## (2) Warp + Claude Code: TUI, Agent Mode, and claude-code-warp

### Running Claude Code CLI in Warp

When you run `claude` in a Warp terminal, Warp auto-detects it and enables enhanced features. Claude Code's TUI is preserved: Warp does NOT replace it. From the official docs at [docs.warp.dev/agent-platform/cli-agents/claude-code/](https://docs.warp.dev/agent-platform/cli-agents/claude-code/):
- Desktop and in-app notifications when Claude Code needs attention
- Ctrl-G opens a rich expanded input editor for composing longer prompts (separate from the Claude Code TUI's own input)
- Inline code review, context sharing, vertical tabs with agent metadata

This works via the [warpdotdev/claude-code-warp](https://github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp) plugin, which uses Claude Code's hook system: SessionStart, Stop, Notification (idle_prompt), PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse hooks. Each hook fires a shell script that emits OSC 777 escape sequences (`\033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<JSON-payload>\007`) to Warp. The JSON payload carries event type, session_id, cwd, query summary, and response summary. Warp renders these as native notifications and updates the vertical tab status indicators.

This integration DOES NOT intercept Claude Code's TUI rendering — it only reads lifecycle events from Claude Code's hook output.

### What "Claude Code TUI" means for alt-screen

With default Claude Code settings (classic renderer, no `/tui fullscreen`), Claude Code outputs directly to the primary scrollback screen as lines. Warp treats this as normal block output. The Warp input editor remains visible at the bottom. Ctrl-G opens the expanded rich input box.

With Claude Code fullscreen mode enabled (`/tui fullscreen` or `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1`), Claude Code DOES send DECSET 1049 and Warp enters `alt_screen_active = true`. In this case, Warp's normal block-based input editor disappears per the logic above. However, the `use_agent_footer` (the CLI agent's Ctrl-G rich input, which is Warp's overlay) CAN appear on top of the alt-screen surface — confirmed by the `is_alt_screen_active && should_render_use_agent_footer` check and by the contrast-adjustment logic in `cli_agent.rs` that specifically handles "alt-screen CLI agent" backgrounds (e.g. OpenCode's inferred BG color).

### Warp native Agent Mode vs Claude Code CLI

| | Claude Code CLI in Warp | Warp Agent Mode ("Oz") |
|---|---|---|
| Who executes | Claude Code process in PTY | Warp's built-in agent |
| Terminal context | Claude Code owns its PTY and TUI | Warp agent sees full terminal session state |
| Input UI | Claude Code's own REPL + Warp's Ctrl-G overlay | Warp's input editor directly |
| Parallelism | One session per tab | Multiple tabs, cloud orchestration |
| Auth | Anthropic API key | Warp subscription |
| Migration guide | [docs.warp.dev/migrate-to-warp/migrate-to-warp-from-claude-code](https://docs.warp.dev/getting-started/migrate-to-warp/migrate-to-warp-from-claude-code/) | — |

---

## (3) Feasibility of a Warp-style separate input box on your libghostty stack

### The key tension (confirmed)

The claim in the context is correct. The fundamental conflict:
- **Line-based shell output**: A fixed-bottom input editor works cleanly. The host reads the PTY byte stream, tracks cursor position, and knows where output ends and the "current input line" begins (via preexec/precmd DCS hooks, just like Warp). The input box floats below all output.
- **Full-screen TUI (vim, htop, btop)**: The app sends DECSET 1049 and writes directly to the alternate screen grid. There is no "input line" — the entire surface belongs to the TUI. The input box MUST disappear. If you fail to detect alt-screen and keep showing an input box overlay, you cover the TUI's content and break keyboard routing (keystrokes go to the overlay rather than to the running process).
- **Claude Code interactive (classic renderer)**: Behaves like a sophisticated line-output program writing to the primary screen. A Warp-style bottom input box would COVER Claude Code's own prompt line. This is architecturally broken for running Claude Code's own REPL.
- **Claude Code fullscreen renderer**: Sends DECSET 1049. Same as vim — your input box must hide.

### What Warp actually does — and the honest lesson

Warp's approach for "input box coexisting with Claude Code":
1. Alt-screen detected → hide standard input editor, render `AltScreenElement` full-height
2. For CLI agent integrations, a separate `use_agent_footer` (Warp's rich input overlay via Ctrl-G) CAN appear on top of the alt-screen surface — but only for the specific case of a known CLI agent (identified by the Warp plugin's OSC notifications), and it adjusts colors against the alt-screen background.
3. For Claude Code fullscreen mode, the Ctrl-G overlay can appear on top of Claude Code's rendered surface — this is the "Warp-style separate input box" experience, but it's not the default UI; it's an opt-in overlay.

### For your libghostty stack specifically

Your surface API is opaque — you get VT stream as-is and action callbacks (COMMAND_FINISHED, PWD, TITLE, PROGRESS) plus `read_text`. You do NOT get a parsed grid.

Options, in order of feasibility:

**A. Shell command input box (line-mode only, feasible)**
Detect alt-screen entry via the DECSET 1049 escape in the raw VT byte stream (look for `\033[?1049h` / `\033[?1049l`). When not in alt-screen, intercept the user's typed line in a native input box before forwarding it to the PTY. On alt-screen entry, hide the native input box and give all input directly to the PTY. This is exactly Warp's mechanism. Warp does this at the VT parser layer via their forked `vte` crate (`warpdotdev/vte` in Cargo.toml).

**B. Claude Code integration via Agent SDK (feasible, bypasses TUI entirely)**
Rather than running `claude` in the PTY and dealing with its TUI, use the Claude Code Agent SDK (`claude --output-format stream-json`) or the MCP interface. Claude Code emits structured JSON events; your native Swift pane receives them and renders them as native UI cards. The user types into a native input box; prompts go directly to the Claude Code process via its stdin. This avoids the entire "TUI owns the screen" problem. This is the architecturally clean path for native-quality Claude Code integration.

**C. Hybrid: shell blocks + agent SDK (Warp's actual 2026 approach)**
For normal shell commands, use approach A (detect alt-screen, show/hide input box). For Claude Code, use approach B (Agent SDK with native input). This is effectively what Warp 2.0's "CLI Agents" feature does — Warp renders Claude Code's TUI in the PTY (with Ctrl-G overlay) while also offering a native agent mode that bypasses the TUI entirely.

**Limitation with libghostty opaque surface**: You can detect DECSET 1049 in the raw byte stream before it reaches the renderer. libghostty will render the alt-screen correctly on its surface. You just need to track the state yourself to show/hide your native input box. The `read_text` action callback won't help here — you need to parse VT escape sequences from the PTY read loop, specifically watching for `\033[?1049h` (enter alt-screen) and `\033[?1049l` (leave alt-screen).

## Adversarial Claim Verdict

**Claim**: "Claude Code never uses alt-screen — its interactive mode stays on the primary screen."
**Verdict**: REFUTED with high confidence.

**Evidence**:
- Official Claude Code docs ([code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen)) state explicitly: "Fullscreen rendering…draws the interface on the terminal's alternate screen buffer, like vim or htop."
- Available since v2.1.89; opt-in via `/tui fullscreen` or `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1`; opt-out via `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1`
- Issue #42670 confirms regression introduced in v2.1.89 where "Claude Code switched to an alternate screen buffer…native terminal scrollback destroyed"
- TerminalModel code comment at `terminal_model.rs:452`: `/// For fullscreen programs like vim. alt_screen: AltScreen,` — and `enter_alt_screen(true)` is called in tests

**Correction**: The claim was historically accurate (pre-v2.1.89, Claude Code wrote only to the primary scrollback). As of v2.1.89+, Claude Code has TWO rendering modes: classic (primary screen, scrollback preserved) and fullscreen (DECSET 1049, alternate buffer, no scrollback). The classic renderer remains the default unless the user explicitly enables fullscreen. So the updated accurate statement is: "Claude Code defaults to the primary screen, but fullscreen mode (opt-in) uses DECSET 1049."

verdict.correction: The accurate statement as of v2.1.89+: Claude Code has TWO rendering modes. (1) Classic renderer (default): outputs line-by-line to the primary scrollback screen, no DECSET 1049 used. (2) Fullscreen renderer (opt-in: /tui fullscreen or CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1): uses DECSET 1049, draws on the alternate screen buffer exactly like vim or htop. To force classic regardless of saved setting: CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1. The pre-v2.1.89 claim (primary screen only) is historically correct but stale.

corpus.summary: Warp provides real but partial "Warp-style input box" features when running Claude Code CLI. The core architecture is: Claude Code runs as its own TUI that owns the PTY stream; Warp overlays an agent toolbar and a Ctrl-G rich-input modal on top, and injects submitted text into Claude Code's PTY via bracketed-paste or a DelayedEnter strategy rather than replacing Claude Code's input line. Warp's own native agent (Oz) is a fully separate product with a true native input box, tool-call cards, and model routing — that is where the Warp-native UX actually lives. The warpdotdev/claude-code-warp plugin is notification-only (OSC 777 sequences); it does not give Claude Code a native Warp input box. Running Claude Code in Warp therefore gives you a hybrid: Claude Code's own TUI stays fully visible and handles input, while Warp adds notifications, session metadata, and the Ctrl-G overlay editor. A fully Warp-native input-box-plus-cards experience for Claude Code requires either using Warp's own agent Oz (which is distinct) or driving Claude Code via the Agent SDK (stream-JSON) in a native pane — not running the Claude Code TUI at all.

points.claim: When you run `claude` inside Warp, Claude Code TUI stays fully in control of the screen — Warp does NOT hide the Claude Code input
points.detail: Warp auto-detects Claude Code via a 'harness' layer that watches the PTY stream for process/OSC signals. Once detected it activates an agent toolbar footer and enables the Ctrl-G rich-input overlay. But Claude Code's own TUI — its input line, spinner, tool-use blocks, diff output — continues to render into the terminal surface exactly as it does in any other terminal. Warp does not intercept or replace Claude Code's render loop. Evidence: (1) Warp issue #9365 (April 2026) explicitly describes 'the input prompt box renders mid-output during long-running TUI sessions (Claude Code)' as a rendering artifact — confirming the two systems are drawing to the same surface independently. (2) Issue #9179 is a feature request that Shift+Tab for Claude Code's own mode-switching (allow/plan/auto) should not be intercepted by Warp's Rich Input. (3) Issue #11644 describes Claude Code input focus being lost inside Warp after brief typing pauses — a focus-management conflict between two input systems. These are all coexistence bugs that would not exist if Warp replaced Claude Code's TUI.

points.claim: Warp's Ctrl-G rich input is a modal overlay that composes a prompt and injects it into Claude Code's PTY — it is not a persistent replacement for Claude Code's own input line
points.detail: Pressing Ctrl-G opens an expanded Warp-native editor modal (multi-line, mouse-aware, @-mention for files/symbols, voice, slash-commands). When you submit, Warp sends the composed text to Claude Code's PTY using one of three strategies depending on the agent and version: 'Inline' (text + carriage return together), 'BracketedPaste' (wrapped in \e[200~ ... \e[201~), or 'DelayedEnter' (text then \r after a delay — explicitly used for Claude Code and Gemini). This means Warp is feeding text to Claude Code's own input mechanism, not bypassing it. Claude Code still renders its own input area; you can also type directly into Claude Code's input without using the Warp overlay. The overlay is an optional convenience layer, not a replacement. Claude Code's docs (terminal-config page, Warp entry) confirm Shift+Enter works natively in Warp without any /terminal-setup, which is consistent with Warp intercepting keystrokes and forwarding them via its harness.

points.claim: Claude Code has two distinct rendering modes: classic (appends to scrollback) and fullscreen (alt-screen buffer, like vim) — and these interact differently with Warp's block model
points.detail: Claude Code's default mode appends output to the terminal scrollback buffer in a scrolling fashion. This is not a true alt-screen / alternate-screen-buffer takeover: Warp can overlay its footer and toolbar on top. Fullscreen mode (opt-in via CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1 or /tui fullscreen, available since v2.1.89) explicitly uses the terminal's alternate screen buffer 'like vim or htop', with a fixed input box at the bottom, mouse capture, and no use of native scrollback. In fullscreen mode Claude Code is unambiguously a full-screen TUI. Warp's issue #9365 describes the classic-mode behavior as problematic: 'Claude Code's alt-screen + streaming-redraw pattern (heavy diff blocks + tool-use blocks streaming while background shells are still running)' causes Warp's input prompt box to render mid-output. This is a conflict between Warp's desire to pin its footer at the bottom and Claude Code's streaming output repaints. In fullscreen mode, Claude Code's alt-screen would suppress Warp's block model for that pane entirely, consistent with how Warp handles vim and htop.

points.claim: Warp's block model and input editor pin-to-bottom coexist with — but do not fully tame — Claude Code's TUI rendering in classic mode; alt-screen (fullscreen mode) suppresses Warp's block chrome
points.detail: In normal shell usage Warp pins its input box at the bottom and groups each command+output into a 'block'. When Claude Code (classic mode) runs, it streams continuous VT output that Warp tries to wrap in a block. Because Claude Code does heavy streaming repaints — diff blocks, tool-use cards — Warp's block segmentation and footer rendering can conflict, producing visual artifacts (issue #9365: prompt box appears mid-output, lines get clipped). In fullscreen mode (alt-screen), the terminal convention is that the application owns the entire screen; Warp follows this by resizing the alt-grid in place and suppressing its own overlaid chrome (block outlines, input footer) for that pane. This is the same behaviour as with vim or htop. The Warp documentation for full-screen apps notes it 'runs alt-grid apps like vim and Emacs in full-screen mode' and supports mouse-event forwarding to the app; there is no mention of the Warp input box remaining visible during full-screen TUI sessions.

points.claim: warpdotdev/claude-code-warp is a Claude Code hooks plugin that delivers notifications and session-state signals to Warp via OSC 777 — it does NOT provide a native Warp input box for Claude Code
points.detail: The plugin (open source, MIT-licensed) maps six Claude Code lifecycle hooks to shell scripts: SessionStart, Stop, Notification (idle_prompt), PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse. Each script builds a JSON payload and calls warp-notify.sh, which emits an OSC 777 escape sequence: \033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<json>\007. Warp parses this to drive its in-app notification center and desktop alerts — showing what Claude is doing, when it finishes, and when it needs permission approval. The plugin also reads the transcript file on Stop to extract the last query and response for the notification body. Critically: the plugin delivers no input-box functionality whatsoever. It is a one-way status/notification bridge. The OSC 777 channel is write-only from Claude Code to Warp. For Claude Code versions >= 2.1.141, the hook output can carry a terminalSequence JSON field instead of writing to /dev/tty directly, making it work in headless/remote contexts too.

points.claim: Warp native agent Oz is entirely distinct from running Claude Code CLI — Oz has a true native UI with model-selector, input toolbelt, tool-call cards, and conversation history built into Warp's own renderer
points.detail: Oz is Warp's built-in coding agent with a fully native UI: Agent Mode surfaces a model selector, voice input, image attachment, slash-commands, conversation history, code-diff viewer, and tool-execution cards as first-class WarpUI components. None of this goes through the VT stream. Oz is multi-model: 65% of its tokens currently route to Anthropic Claude (Opus 4.7, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5 are all available), 35% to other providers (OpenAI GPT-5.x, Google Gemini 3.1, open-source via Fireworks). Warp's case study with Anthropic states 65% of tokens across 40M Warp Agent conversations went to Claude models, and 10M Claude Code CLI sessions have been run in Warp. These are separate usage populations. Users pay Warp AI credits for Oz; they pay Anthropic directly (Max subscription or API key) for Claude Code CLI. There is no way to use a Claude Max subscription inside Oz — Oz uses Warp's own API routing and credit system. Warp does offer 'Bring your own LLM' on Business/Enterprise plans, which can route to Anthropic via your own API key, but this is an enterprise feature.

points.claim: Warp 2.0 Agentic Development Environment native UI (input box + tool cards) is Oz-only — external CLI agents like Claude Code cannot get that native UI in the current architecture
points.detail: Warp 2.0 introduced four product areas: Code (native editor), Agents (Oz + cloud runs), Terminal, and Drive. The native agent UI — model selector, tool-call cards that show each tool invocation, code diff viewer, parallel agent management dashboard — is built for Oz in WarpUI Rust components. Third-party CLI agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, OpenCode) get a different, lighter harness: Warp overlays a footer toolbar (agent utility bar) on their PTY session, detects them via process/OSC signals, and exposes the Ctrl-G rich-input overlay. They do not get native tool-call cards rendered by Warp. Their tool use is shown via Claude Code's own TUI rendering (which Claude Code draws itself into the PTY). To get a fully native Warp-like input box AND tool-call cards for Claude Code functionality, the only architecturally consistent path today is to use Oz — Warp's own agent that can execute the same coding tasks using Claude models under the hood. Warp's migration page explicitly frames running Claude Code vs using Oz as 'two viable paths', not one being a superset of the other.

points.claim: Feasibility assessment for the user's remote-coding tool: a Warp-style separate input box for shell commands is architecturally straightforward; for Claude Code interactive TUI it requires bypassing the TUI entirely via Agent SDK (stream-JSON)
points.detail: The user's tool has an opaque libghostty surface (VT stream passthrough, action callbacks for COMMAND_FINISHED/PWD/TITLE/PROGRESS, read_text, no parsed grid). For shell commands in line-discipline mode: a separate native input box sending typed text to the PTY is exactly what Warp does for its block-mode input. This is feasible. The input box feeds stdin to the shell's line discipline; the output appears in the scrollback surface. The mode switch for TUI apps (vim, htop, Claude Code fullscreen) is the tension point: when a program enters alt-screen, it owns the entire terminal surface, and the input box must yield. Warp does this by detecting Mode::AltScreen in its vte fork and suppressing its overlay chrome for that pane. The user's libghostty stack has the same constraint: alt-screen takeover from any TUI (including Claude Code fullscreen mode) means the input box must hide. For Claude Code specifically, if the goal is Warp-style input box AND tool-call cards alongside Claude Code, the only viable path is Agent SDK (stream-JSON output from `claude --output-format stream-json`), parsing tool-use events natively in the host app, and building a native input pane that sends prompts to Claude Code over stdin — never spawning Claude Code's TUI at all. This is what Warp would need to do to give Claude Code a fully native UI, and what Warp's own Oz agent already does for its own model routing.

verdict.claim: The 'DelayedEnter' PTY injection strategy for Claude Code (from DeepWiki/Warp docs) — this is sourced from DeepWiki's AI synthesis of the Warp codebase, not directly from readable source code.
verdict.evidence: Direct reading of the full AGPL Warp source at warpdotdev/warp (shallow clone, HEAD a44b703, May 2026) confirms: there is no `DelayedEnter`, `delayed_enter`, `harness_support.rs` (the file that exists is `harness_support.rs` but it is a CLI dispatch file for the Oz agent platform, not a PTY injection strategy), nor any PTY-injection mechanism for Claude Code in the harness. The actual mechanism found in `app/src/ai/agent_sdk/driver/harness/claude_code.rs` is: Warp writes the prompt to a temp file and launches Claude Code CLI with `claude --session-id <uuid> --dangerously-skip-permissions < '<prompt_path>'` (stdin redirect from temp file). There is no Enter-key injection or PTY byte injection. The "DelayedEnter" term does not appear anywhere in the codebase. The DeepWiki page for warpdotdev/claude-code-warp also contains no mention of DelayedEnter or PTY injection — it accurately describes the OSC 777 hook system. The original claim was sourced from an earlier AI synthesis, not from readable code.
verdict.correction: The actual Claude Code integration strategy in Warp's AGPL source (as of HEAD a44b703, May 2026) is: (1) FOR OZ/AGENT MODE: Warp writes the prompt to a temp file, then launches `claude --session-id <uuid> --dangerously-skip-permissions < '<prompt_path>'` via `TerminalDriver.execute_command`. This is a non-interactive stdin-redirect launch, NOT PTY injection. Claude Code runs in the alt-screen as a TUI and Warp's block-list input editor is hidden while it is active. (2) FOR RUNNING CLAUDE CODE INTERACTIVELY (typing `claude` in shell): Claude Code takes the alt-screen. Warp's `is_input_box_visible()` returns `false` when `model.is_alt_screen_active() && !block.is_agent_in_control() && !block.is_agent_tagged_in()`. The exception is when `has_active_cli_agent_input_session()` is true — this is Warp's 'rich input' overlay (Ctrl-G), which is a separate Warp-native overlay that communicates with the running CLI via the claude-code-warp hooks (OSC 777 escape sequences), NOT by injecting into the PTY. The hooks plugin communicates structured JSON via OSC 777 sequences written to /dev/tty. Full research findings follow in the answer body.

verdict.claim: Warp's Mode::AltScreen detection suppresses overlay chrome — the terminal's block-mode UI (input editor, blocks) is hidden and replaced by the AltScreenElement when an alt-screen program runs.
verdict.correction: The DeepWiki synthesis claim was directionally correct but understated the mechanism. The suppression is not just "Mode::AltScreen detection suppresses overlay chrome" as a vague rendering flag — it is a named four-state enum `TerminalInputState { AltScreen, InputEditor, LongRunningCommand, NotBootstrapped }` computed from `is_alt_screen_active()`, which gates `is_input_box_visible()`. The full block list is also suppressed; `AltScreenElement` replaces it. The claim is confirmed but the actual mechanism is more specific: it is a render-path branch keyed on `TerminalInputState`, not a generic overlay-chrome suppression flag. One important nuance the original claim missed: the CLI agent footer (toolbelt, Ctrl-G rich input) is explicitly kept alive even during AltScreen — it is not suppressed. Only the normal Warp input editor (the block-mode input box) is hidden.

corpus.summary: Warp's Rust source code (now AGPL on GitHub) reveals the exact mechanism: alt-screen is detected by parsing the VT stream (DECSET 1049/1047/47 → `Mode::SwapScreen`) inside the ANSI handler, setting an internal boolean `alt_screen_active`, and then the render function switches the entire output area from `BlockListElement` (block-mode + input box) to `AltScreenElement` (full-surface raw grid) while also calling `is_input_box_visible()` — which returns `false` when `alt_screen_active` is true — to hide the input box. The libghostty embedded surface API (`ghostty.h`) exposes only a single `ghostty_runtime_action_cb` callback that fires for all actions; there is NO dedicated alt-screen state-change callback and no `ghostty_surface_get_active_screen()` function. The `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN` query exists only in the separate `libghostty-vt` sub-library header (`include/ghostty/vt/terminal.h`), not in the main `ghostty.h` surface API. For a host using libghostty in opaque-surface mode, the only reliable way to detect alt-screen entry/exit is to intercept the raw VT byte stream and parse `ESC[?1049h`/`ESC[?1049l` before handing bytes to libghostty — or to use the `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE` and related action callbacks as indirect signals, or to poll `read_text` heuristically. For Claude Code specifically, running its interactive TUI means it uses the normal screen (not alt-screen), owns the full PTY in raw/char mode, and Warp's "rich input" integration with Claude Code works via OSC 777 plugin hooks and a separately-opened Ctrl-G rich editor overlay — not by hijacking the TUI. The clean architecture for your stack is: (1) shell block mode = native SwiftUI input box, bytes sent to PTY on Enter, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED` callback draws block boundary; (2) TUI passthrough = detect alt-screen by parsing your own byte pipe before it reaches libghostty, hide the input box, forward all keypresses raw; (3) Claude Code = drive via Agent SDK (`claude -p --output-format stream-json`) into a native SwiftUI pane rather than running its TUI.

points.claim: Warp's input box hides on alt-screen entry — confirmed from AGPL source code
points.detail: Warp's terminal model ANSI handler in `app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs` matches `ansi::Mode::SwapScreen` inside `fn set_mode()` and calls `self.enter_alt_screen()`, which sets `self.alt_screen_active = true` and emits a `TerminalModeSwapped(TerminalMode::AltScreen)` event. The main render function in `view.rs` (around line 26916) checks `is_alt_screen_active` to choose between rendering `AltScreenElement` (full raw grid) or `BlockListElement` (blocks + input box). The function `is_input_box_visible()` (line 7659) returns `false` when `model.is_alt_screen_active()` is true — with one exception: if a CLI agent is flagged as being 'in control' (e.g., active Claude Code session with rich input open). This is the exact mechanism for hiding the input box when vim, btop, or any TUI runs.

points.claim: Warp detects alt-screen by parsing the VT stream itself, not via a callback from an underlying terminal library
points.detail: Warp implements its own ANSI handler (`impl ansi::Handler for TerminalModel`) and parses the PTY byte stream directly. When DECSET 1047/1049 arrives, the ANSI stream handler calls `enter_alt_screen()` synchronously inside the parse loop. There is no underlying terminal emulator library that fires an 'alt-screen entered' callback to Warp; Warp is the parser. This is important for the libghostty stack: since libghostty processes the VT stream internally and only fires high-level `ghostty_runtime_action_cb` callbacks for app-level events (title change, pwd, command-finished, etc.), the libghostty surface host does NOT get an alt-screen notification through the standard action callback interface.
sources: ghostty-org/ghostty include/ghostty.h:1000-1005 (ghostty_runtime_config_s — only action_cb, no alt-screen callback)

points.claim: The libghostty main surface API (ghostty.h) has NO alt-screen state-change callback and NO query function for screen mode
points.detail: The complete `ghostty_runtime_config_s` struct in `ghostty.h` has exactly six function pointers: `wakeup_cb`, `action_cb`, `read_clipboard_cb`, `confirm_read_clipboard_cb`, `write_clipboard_cb`, `close_surface_cb`. The `action_cb` fires for all high-level actions: `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PWD`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_DESKTOP_NOTIFICATION`, and ~30 UI actions (split, fullscreen, tab, color change, etc.). None of the action tags is `GHOSTTY_ACTION_ALT_SCREEN_ENTERED` or similar. The public function list (`ghostty_surface_*`) has no `ghostty_surface_get_active_screen()` or mode-query function. The `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN` enum value (which can query primary vs alternate screen) exists ONLY in the `libghostty-vt` sub-library header (`include/ghostty/vt/terminal.h`) which is a separate, lower-level library for embedding just the VT parser — not the full GPU-rendered surface. If your stack uses the full surface API (opaque libghostty surface that renders to Metal), `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN` is not accessible.
sources: ghostty-org/ghostty include/ghostty.h:884-951 (full ghostty_action_tag_e enum — no alt-screen entry action)
ghostty-org/ghostty include/ghostty/vt/modes.h:71-90 (GHOSTTY_MODE_ALT_SCREEN_* mode constants — libghostty-vt only)

points.claim: Warp's 'Warp-style input box' for the block model works by sending the command bytes to the PTY, intercepting the shell prompt, and using semantic prompt markers (OSC 133/633) for block boundaries — not by disabling shell line-editing
points.detail: Warp's `BlockListElement` and block model rely on the shell emitting OSC 133/633 shell integration escape sequences (`precmd` / `preexec` hooks) to identify where each prompt starts and command output ends. The `block_list.rs` model tracks `PrecmdValue`, `PreexecValue`, and `CommandFinishedValue` (from `ansi.rs`). The input editor sends the typed command to the PTY as bytes followed by a newline — the shell then echoes it and executes it. Block boundaries are drawn around the {prompt + command + output} tuple. For COMMAND_FINISHED, libghostty also exposes `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED` (struct: `exit_code: int16_t`, `duration: uint64_t`) through the action callback, which provides an alternative signal for block completion without needing OSC 133 parsing.
sources: warpdotdev/warp app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs (ansi handler — PrecmdValue, PreexecValue handling)

points.claim: Warp does NOT give you a Warp-style input box when running Claude Code's interactive TUI — it detects the CLI agent and shows a separate 'rich input' overlay (Ctrl-G), but Claude Code's own TUI is still the primary interactive surface
points.detail: Warp's `cli_agent.rs` and `cli_agent_sessions/` code track named CLI agents (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex, etc.) by detecting the top-level command name. When Claude Code runs interactively, it does NOT typically enter alt-screen (it renders its TUI on the normal scrollback screen using character-addressed VT sequences, not DECSET 1049). So Warp's block-mode input box remains visible initially. Warp enriches the experience via: (1) the `warpdotdev/claude-code-warp` plugin hooks (OSC 777 → `warp://cli-agent` URI with JSON payload) for status notifications, and (2) a 'rich input' editor opened with Ctrl-G (`CLIAgentInputState::Open`) that suspends the regular input and lets you compose a longer prompt that is then piped to Claude Code's stdin. The Warp native Agent Mode (built-in, using Warp's own model) is architecturally separate from running Claude Code CLI. The claude-code-warp hooks fire on: `SessionStart`, `Stop`, `Notification(idle_prompt)`, `PermissionRequest`, `UserPromptSubmit`, `PostToolUse`.

points.claim: For your libghostty stack: detecting alt-screen requires intercepting the PTY byte stream before it reaches libghostty and parsing DECSET 1049/1047/47 yourself
points.detail: Since the libghostty main surface API provides no alt-screen callback and no mode-query function, your Swift host must sit in the PTY byte pipeline. The reliable approach: in the read loop that receives bytes from the NetBird TCP PTY stream, scan for the known sequences `ESC [ ? 1049 h` (enter alt-screen), `ESC [ ? 1049 l` (exit), plus the legacy 1047/47 variants before forwarding bytes to `ghostty_surface_text()`. This is a small, well-defined parser — the sequences are fixed-length and unambiguous. When detected, set a Swift-side boolean `altScreenActive`, show/hide the SwiftUI input box, and change the key-forwarding mode. You do NOT need to parse the full VT stream; only these six escape sequences. Alternative (weaker): watch for the `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED` action callback as a proxy signal that the previous command (which may have been a TUI) exited — but this has false-negative cases (TUI programs that finish commands without entering alt-screen) and false-positive cases. There is no heuristic based on `read_text` that is reliable enough.
sources: ghostty-org/ghostty include/ghostty.h:1100-1130 (ghostty_surface_text — bytes in; no mode change callback out)

points.claim: For Claude Code specifically on your stack: the recommended approach is Agent SDK (stream-json headless), NOT running its interactive TUI through libghostty
points.detail: Claude Code's interactive TUI renders using character-addressed VT sequences to the normal screen (not alt-screen for most states), drives raw mode input, and owns the full visible area. Embedding this in a libghostty surface means you get a rendered pixel surface you cannot structurally decompose — you cannot extract 'the current tool call' or 'the current assistant response' from it without OCR or `read_text` heuristics. The Agent SDK approach (`claude -p --output-format stream-json --verbose --include-partial-messages`) streams NDJSON events: `system/init`, `assistant` (with `text_delta` events), `tool_use`, `tool_result`, `result`. A Swift native pane can subscribe to this stream, render each message type as a card (diff viewer, file edit preview, bash output block), and feed user input via the process's stdin. This is exactly the architecture Warp uses for its built-in native Agent Mode (separate from the CLI agent passthrough). Starting June 15, 2026, Agent SDK credits are metered separately from interactive usage on subscription plans.

## 1. How Warp's Input Editor Works and Coexists with Full-Screen TUI Programs

### Architecture: Block Model + Separate Input Editor

Warp's [block model](https://docs.warp.dev/terminal/blocks/block-basics/) groups each command and its output into a discrete "block" — a shell-hook-separated unit (precmd/preexec). The terminal is implemented as three distinct grids per command: one for the prompt, one for command input, and one for command output. The separate input editor at the bottom is a full text-editor view (rope/SumTree-backed, Operation-based CRDT) that sits independent of the output area.

Source architecture detail from the [how-warp-works blog](https://www.warp.dev/blog/how-warp-works): "Warp separates commands into blocks, and each block consists of three grids: one for the prompt, one for the command input, and one for the command output."

### What Happens with Full-Screen TUI Apps

When a program activates the alternate screen buffer (DECSET 1049 — vim, htop, btop, etc.), Warp **does not hide the input editor via a mode switch in the classic sense**. Instead:

- Warp maintains an `AltScreen` struct distinct from the `BlockList`. This is confirmed by the [open-source Rust repo](https://github.com/warpdotdev/warp) (AGPL, public since April 2026): `app/src/terminal/model/terminal_model.rs` contains `alt_screen: AltScreen` (with comment "For fullscreen programs like vim") and `alt_screen_active: bool` fields.
- Sourcegraph search of the repo shows `is_alt_screen_active()` is checked in multiple rendering paths. The check at [`app/src/terminal/input.rs` line 11402–11404](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/warpdotdev/Warp/-/blob/app/src/terminal/input.rs?L11402) reads: `!model.is_alt_screen_active() && model.block_list().active_block().is_command_grid_active()` — this gates the block-list input editor behavior specifically on `!is_alt_screen_active()`.
- The [full-screen apps docs page](https://docs.warp.dev/terminal/more-features/full-screen-apps/) confirms: "Warp runs alt-grid apps like Vim and Emacs in full-screen mode" and "opening full-screen programs like vim and emacs are themselves instances of this same grid, just with expanded dimensions."
- The Warp input editor (bottom bar) **disappears when alt-screen is active**; the alt-screen app owns the full drawing surface. Mouse events route to the alt-screen app via ANSI sequences. Holding Shift sends the mouse event to Warp instead.

**Key mechanism in `cli_agent.rs`**: When Claude Code's TUI is running AND its CLI-agent session is open (`CLIAgentSessionsModel::is_input_open()`), Warp renders `render_cli_agent_input` — its own rich input — rather than the standard block-list input. If `is_alt_screen_active()` is true, Warp samples the alt-screen's background color to match styling. This means Warp's CLI agent integration layer (Ctrl-G rich input, agent footer) explicitly tracks the alt-screen state.

**Summary for the builder**: The block-list input editor only renders when `!is_alt_screen_active()`. Full-screen TUI programs get the whole surface. No explicit "passthrough" mode — it is simply a different rendering path.

## 2. Warp + Claude Code: What the User Actually Sees

### Claude Code running as TUI in Warp (without special integration)

Claude Code in its **default rendering mode** (no `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1` / `tui` setting) outputs inline to the scrollback — standard VT streaming with no alt-screen. Warp's block model does not break this: Claude Code's inline output lands in a normal Warp output block. The Warp input editor at the bottom is NOT replaced; it shows after each Claude Code "turn" ends (when Claude Code is waiting for input, it is technically waiting for a line via the PTY's cooked/canonical mode, so Warp's line-editor is still in play).

When Claude Code's **fullscreen mode** is active (`CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN` is not set, and `/tui fullscreen` or `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1` is used), Claude Code issues DECSET 1049 and takes over the screen. In this mode, Warp's block-list input editor disappears (per the `!is_alt_screen_active()` guard above) and Claude Code owns the full terminal surface — including its own fixed-bottom input box inside the alt-screen buffer.

### Warp's CLI-Agent Integration (the valuable part)

Warp has [dedicated first-class integration for Claude Code](https://docs.warp.dev/agent-platform/cli-agents/claude-code/) that goes beyond TUI detection:

- **Auto-detection**: Warp recognizes the `claude` command and activates a "CLI agent session" (verified via `CLIAgentSessionsModel` in the Warp Rust codebase).
- **Ctrl-G rich input**: Pressing Ctrl-G opens an expanded multi-line input editor overlay — Warp composing the prompt before sending it to Claude Code's PTY. This is Warp's own input UI, not Claude Code's TUI.
- **OSC 777 notification bridge** ([`warpdotdev/claude-code-warp`](https://github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp)): Claude Code hooks (SessionStart, Stop, Notification/idle_prompt, PermissionRequest, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse) fire shell scripts that emit `\033]777;notify;warp://cli-agent;<JSON-payload>\007` to Warp. Warp parses the JSON payload (protocol version, agent, event, session_id, cwd, query, response) and drives native notifications and session tab metadata. Claude Code ≥ 2.1.141 uses `{terminalSequence: ...}` JSON output on stdout; older versions write directly to `/dev/tty`.
- **Vertical tabs with agent metadata**: Running agents show status (idle/running/blocked) in Warp's tab bar.
- **Code review panel**: Warp can show a diff/review UI alongside the Claude Code session.
- **Session sharing**: Remote control for collaboration.

**Critical design point**: In Warp's CLI-agent integration, Claude Code's TUI still runs as-is — Warp wraps around it with metadata and UI chrome. The Ctrl-G input is Warp intercepting before-submit, composing the prompt in a richer editor, then writing it to the PTY. This is not replacing Claude Code's TUI; it is augmenting the input pathway.

### Warp's Native Agent Mode vs. Claude Code CLI

The [Warp vs Claude Code guide](https://docs.warp.dev/guides/agent-workflows/warp-vs-claude-code/) distinguishes them cleanly:

- **Warp native agents**: Fully integrated in Warp's UI. Agents see Warp's block output, have access to Warp Drive (shared knowledge store), and run in Warp's agentic framework. Scored #1 on Terminal-bench (52%) and top-3 on SWE-bench Verified (75.8%).
- **Claude Code in Warp**: Claude Code CLI is a guest running in a Warp terminal. Warp provides the "CLI agent session" UI layer (metadata, notifications, rich input) but Claude Code's own rendering owns the conversation display.

The [Warp 2.0 / ADE blog post](https://www.warp.dev/blog/reimagining-coding-agentic-development-environment) (June 2025) describes Warp 2.0 as having four integrated capabilities: Code, Agents, Terminal, Drive. "Warp is the platform and not a CLI-app running within the terminal" — enabling richer UX than traditional CLI agents.

## 3. Claude Code Alt-Screen: The Adversarial Verification

**Claim to verify**: Claude Code does NOT typically use alt-screen (DECSET 1049) during normal operation.

**Verdict: CONFIRMED** with important nuance.

### Default mode (no special flags): Does NOT use alt-screen

Claude Code's default "inline" renderer streams VT output to the normal scrollback buffer. It does NOT issue DECSET 1049. The [Week 14 "What's New" digest](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/whats-new/2026-w14) describes v2.1.89's flicker-free rendering as: "Opt into a new **alt-screen renderer** with virtualized scrollback. [...] Unset `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER` to roll back." This wording confirms alt-screen was OPT-IN at v2.1.89.

### Fullscreen mode: DOES use alt-screen

The [fullscreen docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/fullscreen) state explicitly: "It draws the interface on the **terminal's alternate screen buffer, like `vim` or `htop`**." And: "Because the conversation lives in the **alternate screen buffer** instead of your terminal's scrollback, a few things work differently."

Env var `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1` (added in v2.1.132) opts out of the fullscreen renderer.

### Is fullscreen now the default?

This is uncertain. The docs still say "Fullscreen rendering is an opt-in research preview" (as of the current live docs). However, v2.1.132 added an opt-OUT variable, implying it may have become on-by-default for some users or configurations. The changelog evidence suggests:
- v2.1.89: alt-screen added as opt-in (`CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1`)
- v2.1.110: `/tui` command added; fullscreen remains opt-in  
- v2.1.132: `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1` opt-out added (implying gradual rollout to default)
- v2.1.121 changelog: "Fixed scrollback duplication in **non-fullscreen mode**" — treating non-fullscreen as a named mode suggests fullscreen may have become default by then

**Conservative conclusion**: In a fresh install today, the fullscreen/alt-screen mode may be on by default (based on the opt-out env var in v2.1.132), but this needs empirical verification by running `claude` in a terminal with VT logging. The `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ALTERNATE_SCREEN=1` env var is the definitive escape hatch.

**What this means for alt-screen detection**: If a user has NOT set `CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER=1` (or is on a version before the fullscreen rollout), Claude Code uses inline rendering — NO DECSET 1049, NO alt-screen activation. The alt-screen detection approach would NOT catch it. If fullscreen is active, it DOES use alt-screen and the detection would work.

**Empirical test**: Run `script -q /dev/null claude 2>&1 | xxd | grep -A2 "1049"` to check whether DECSET 1049 (`\x1b[?1049h`) is emitted during startup.
