## 4. Feasibility of a Warp-Style Input Box on Your libghostty Stack

### For shell commands (block/line mode): Feasible

The Warp model for shell commands is straightforward:
1. Shell is in cooked/canonical mode (line-by-line PTY input)
2. Host terminal intercepts keystrokes before they go to the PTY
3. Renders its own input box (native UI, not VT-rendered)
4. On Enter: sends the composed line to the PTY
5. Shell preexec/precmd hooks (or OSC sequences) mark block boundaries in the VT stream

On your libghostty stack: libghostty's surface API is opaque (renders VT stream as-is; host gets action callbacks COMMAND_FINISHED/PWD/TITLE/PROGRESS + read_text, not a parsed grid). This means you cannot inject a native input box at the VT level the way Warp does from inside its own renderer. HOWEVER: you can implement a parallel input layer:
- Intercept PTY input at the host/PTY owner level (you own the PTY)
- Render a native SwiftUI/UIKit input box below/outside the libghostty surface
- When the user submits, write the line to the PTY master fd yourself
- Use OSC sequences (like Warp's preexec/precmd hooks, or OSC 133 A/C markers for shell integration) to correlate input blocks with output blocks in the VT stream

This is the block-mode case and is architecturally clean.

### For Claude Code interactive TUI: Fundamentally Different Problem

**If Claude Code is in inline rendering mode (no alt-screen)**: Claude Code reads line-by-line from PTY stdin (canonical mode). Your native input box can intercept and compose multi-line prompts, then write them to the PTY. This works cleanly. You see the Claude Code output stream in the ghostty surface above, and your native input box below composes prompts. This is essentially what Warp's Ctrl-G does.

**If Claude Code is in fullscreen mode (with alt-screen)**: Claude Code issues DECSET 1049, switches to alternate screen, and owns the entire terminal surface including its own fixed-bottom input box. In this mode, Claude Code's input box is rendered INSIDE the VT stream. Your native input box outside the ghostty surface would be redundant with Claude Code's own input box. Additionally, Claude Code in fullscreen mode reads character-by-character (raw/cbreak mode), not line-by-line — so any interception of typed characters before they reach the PTY would break Claude Code's internal TUI. The alt-screen detection approach from the Warp source (`!is_alt_screen_active()`) is the right signal: when Claude Code enters fullscreen mode, the host should yield the input surface to Claude Code.

### The Real Alternative: Agent SDK approach

For a genuine "separate input box + output cards" UX with Claude Code (not running its TUI at all), the path is:
- Run Claude Code via `claude -p` (headless/stream-json mode) or the [Agent SDK](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/sdk)
- Drive it programmatically: send prompts via stdin/API, receive structured JSON events on stdout
- Render the conversation in native UI panes (tool call cards, diff views, etc.)
- Your native input box is THE input — Claude Code has no TUI
- This is what Warp's native Agent Mode does (not the CLI-agent integration)

The hook-based approach ([warpdotdev/claude-code-warp](https://github.com/warpdotdev/claude-code-warp)) augments the TUI path; the Agent SDK approach replaces it entirely.

### The Key Tension (honestly resolved)

The Warp input editor for shell commands works because the shell is in canonical/cooked mode — the PTY buffers input line-by-line and Warp intercepts before the line is sent. A separate native input box works here.

For Claude Code interactive TUI (especially fullscreen mode), the TUI is in raw/cbreak mode and owns the input surface. A "Warp-style box OUTSIDE it" cannot coexist without either (a) breaking Claude Code's key handling or (b) not being truly separate — just a layer that pre-composes and then dumps text into Claude Code's TUI input.

The honest path to a Warp-2.0-style rich input experience is the Agent SDK / headless approach, not the TUI overlay approach.

verdict.evidence:
Sourced directly from ghostty-org/ghostty at commit 0f7cd84b:

1. vt.h IS the libghostty-vt umbrella header (lines 1-154), and it explicitly includes vt/terminal.h at line 132. GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN = 6 is defined at vt/terminal.h line 666, with output type GhosttyTerminalScreen* (PRIMARY=0 / ALTERNATE=1 at lines 227-232). So the constant exists and vt.h is the correct top-level include for it.

2. However: the full surface API (ghostty.h, the embedded/external-backend API) does NOT expose ghostty_terminal_data or GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN. A complete scan of ghostty.h's published API (lines 1063-1181) reveals no function that takes a GhosttyTerminalDataTag argument or returns GhosttyTerminalScreen. The surface API only delivers screen-state information indirectly, via the action callback (ghostty_runtime_action_cb) with events: GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE, GHOSTTY_ACTION_PWD, GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED, GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT, GHOSTTY_ACTION_SCROLLBAR — none of which is an active-screen query.

3. libghostty-vt is a standalone library (zero-dependency, its own build artifact) that you instantiate by creating a ghostty_terminal_t yourself, feed VT bytes via ghostty_terminal_vt_write(), and then query with ghostty_terminal_data(term, GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN, &screen, sizeof(screen)). It is architecturally separate from the surface API: the surface API owns the terminal internally and does not expose a handle to query.

4. Whether libghostty-vt is linked into a given external-backend build is build-system-dependent. The two libraries are separate artifacts. An iOS/macOS app using the external-backend patch that only links against the surface-API dylib/static lib (ghostty.h) does NOT automatically get the vt sub-library API, and cannot call ghostty_terminal_data(). The vt.h API note explicitly says the API is not yet stable and breaking changes are expected.

CORRECTION CONCLUSION: The claim is confirmed with a critical nuance. GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_ACTIVE_SCREEN exists and is in vt.h (via vt/terminal.h) as claimed. But it is NOT reachable through the surface API (ghostty.h / external-backend path). To use it, you must separately link libghostty-vt, instantiate your own terminal object, and feed it the raw VT byte stream yourself — which means running a parallel terminal state machine alongside the opaque surface renderer. The surface API's action callback does not surface active-screen state directly; it only provides TITLE, PWD, COMMAND_FINISHED, PROGRESS, and SCROLLBAR events.

The correction: it is unreachable via the surface API (ghostty.h). To detect alt-screen from an external-backend build, you must (a) separately link libghostty-vt, (b) instantiate a ghostty_terminal_t yourself, (c) feed it the raw PTY byte stream in parallel with the surface renderer, and (d) call ghostty_terminal_data(). This is a significant extra integration burden. The surface API action callback — which is what the external-backend patch wires to — delivers only TITLE/PWD/COMMAND_FINISHED/PROGRESS/SCROLLBAR; there is no GHOSTTY_ACTION_ACTIVE_SCREEN event in ghostty_action_tag_e.

verdict.evidence: Warp does NOT use OSC 133 for block boundaries. It uses its own custom escape-sequence protocol (originally DCS, migrated to custom OSC codes on Windows because ConPTY did not forward unrecognized DCS). The shell-side mechanism is precmd/preexec hooks (native in zsh/fish, polyfilled via bash-preexec in bash) that emit JSON payloads wrapped in Warp's own escape sequences — not the standard ECMA-48 OSC 133 A/B/C/D sequences. In fact, the issue tracker shows that when a user manually inserts standard OSC 133 "A" markers in PROMPT_COMMAND, Warp breaks (command output disappears), confirming Warp does not parse OSC 133 as a block signal — it is actually confused by it. For the remote-host question: Warp's documentation states that "all core terminal features — the input editor, completions, blocks, history search — work the same as they do locally" even without the SSH extension installed. The SSH extension adds the file tree, native diffs, and reliable completions, but it is not a prerequisite for blocks or the input editor. The separate input-box architecture is implemented at the Warp app layer (keystrokes are buffered in the app and sent to PTY only on Enter), not signaled by shell hooks, which is why it works over SSH regardless of what is installed on the remote host. The partial truth in the claim: Warp's *full* block metadata (git state, pwd annotation, exit code) does depend on the shell-side bootstrap hooks being registered. Without them, Warp can still show a separate input box and catch command boundaries via the precmd/preexec DCS events if Warpification runs, but if neither Warpification nor the SSH extension installs successfully, Warp degrades gracefully rather than failing entirely for the input editor. OSC 133 is specifically NOT the mechanism in any case.
verdict.correction: Warp uses its own proprietary DCS/OSC escape-sequence protocol with JSON payloads, driven by precmd/preexec shell hooks — not OSC 133. Warp is actually broken by OSC 133 when users manually inject it. The input editor (the separate input box) is an app-layer feature that buffers keystrokes in Warp itself and sends the whole command to the PTY on Enter; this does not depend on any shell hook. Full block metadata (exit code, pwd, git state) does require the precmd/preexec hooks to be registered, which Warpification installs; without Warpification the relevant fallback is Warp's own SSH extension (not OSC 133 nor a COMMAND_FINISHED timing hack). The correct fallback statement is: without Warp's own shell-side bootstrap scripts you lose rich block metadata and command-boundary detection but retain the input editor; the COMMAND_FINISHED action callback is a libghostty-specific concept unrelated to Warp's design.
